candidates.label: Real Estate Operator
candidates.definition: A Real Estate Operator is a professional or entity responsible for the day-to-day management, maintenance, and operations of real estate properties. This role includes overseeing property functions, ensuring tenant satisfaction, handling leasing activities, and maintaining the physical condition of the property.

candidates.label: Real Estate Developer
candidates.definition: A Real Estate Developer is a professional or company that acquires land, finances real estate deals, builds or renovates properties, and arranges for their sale or lease. This role involves various stages of development, including market research, site selection, obtaining permits, and managing construction projects.

candidates.label: Real Estate Investor
candidates.definition: Entities or individuals that invest in real property, either directly or through a real estate investment trust (REIT).

candidates.label: Real Property Law
candidates.definition: Law that governs the various forms of ownership and tenancy in real property.

candidates.label: Estate
candidates.definition: An Estate refers to all the possessions, property, and legal rights owned by an individual, including land, tenements, and personal assets. It also encompasses the total assets and liabilities left by a person at death, which are subject to distribution according to the terms of a will or the laws of intestacy.

candidates.label: Real Estate Asset
candidates.definition: Real estate asset type

candidates.label: Real Estate Documents
candidates.definition: Real Estate Documents are written or recorded representations of property ownership, transfer, or use, including but not limited to deeds, mortgages, and leases.

hierarchy_path.label: Document Collections by Areas of Law

candidates.label: Real Estate Lease
candidates.definition: A Real Estate Lease is a contract wherein the owner (lessor) grants the right to occupy and use their property to another party (lessee) for a specified period in exchange for rental payments.

candidates.label: Real Estate Sublease
candidates.definition: A Real Estate Sublease is an agreement where the original tenant (sublessor) rents out the property to another tenant (sublessee), either for the entirety or a portion of the remaining lease term.

candidates.label: Real Estate Commission
candidates.definition: A real estate commission is a fee paid to real estate agents or brokers for their services in facilitating the sale or purchase of property. It is typically a percentage of the final sale price and compensates the agent for their efforts in marketing, negotiating, and closing the real estate transaction

candidates.label: Real Estate Industry
candidates.definition: Industries in the Real Estate group are primarily engaged in renting or leasing real estate to others; managing real estate for others; selling, buying, or renting real estate for others; and providing other real estate related services, such as appraisal services. This subsector includes equity Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITs) that are primarily engaged in leasing buildings, dwellings, or other real estate property to others.

hierarchy_path.label: Real Estate, Rental and Leasing Industry

candidates.label: Real Estate Fraud
candidates.definition: Real Estate Fraud: A type of fraud claim that involves deceitful or dishonest practices related to the buying, selling, or leasing of real property, which may result in property or premises liability claims.

candidates.label: Trespass to Real Property
candidates.definition: The concept of Trespass to Real Property refers to the intentional and unauthorized entry onto someone else's land or property, causing damage or interference with the owner's right to exclusive possession.

candidates.label: Criminal Trespass
candidates.definition: To unlawfully enter land, a dwelling, or other real property.

candidates.label: Bequest
candidates.definition: A Bequest is a provision in a will or trust that designates the transfer of specific assets, property, or money to a particular beneficiary upon the death of the testator. It ensures that the intended recipients receive the designated items as part of the estate distribution.

candidates.label: Land Settlement
candidates.definition: Land settlement refers to the process where individuals or groups occupy and establish a community or settlement on a specific tract of land, often for agricultural, residential, or developmental purposes. This concept can encompass the historical settling of new territories, as well as modern real estate transactions where land is allocated and developed.

candidates.label: Disinheritance
candidates.definition: Disinheritance is the legal act of deliberately excluding an heir from receiving property or assets from a deceased person's estate, typically through the terms specified in a will or trust. This action can be taken for various personal, financial, or relational reasons, and must comply with legal statutes to be effective.

candidates.label: M&A F6 Real Property Practice
candidates.definition: M&A F6 Real Property Practice refers to the legal work performed by a Legal Services Provider in the course of a merger and acquisition transaction that involves real estate property.
synonyms: M&A F6 Prática de Propriedade Imobiliária
M&A F6 פרקטיקת נכסי נדלן אמיתיים
Práctica de propiedad inmobiliaria real M&A F6
Práctica inmobiliaria de propiedad real M&A F6
एम एंड ए एफ 6 रीयल प्रॉपर्टी प्रैक्टिस का एम एंड ए

hierarchy_path.label: M&A F Regulatory and Specialty Matters Practice

candidates.label: Estate
candidates.definition: An Estate refers to all the possessions, property, and legal rights owned by an individual, including land, tenements, and personal assets. It also encompasses the total assets and liabilities left by a person at death, which are subject to distribution according to the terms of a will or the laws of intestacy.

candidates.label: Employment Transactions Practice
candidates.definition: Employment Transactions Practice refers to the area of law that involves the structuring, negotiation, and documentation of agreements related to employment relationships. This includes contracts, compensation plans, and other legal frameworks governing the employee-employer dynamic.

candidates.label: Property Rights and Transactions Law
candidates.definition: Law relating to the acquisition and transfer of real property.
synonyms: Derecho de Propiedad y Ley de Transacciones
Direito de Propriedade e Lei de Transações
Droit des droits de propriété et des transactions

candidates.label: Estate
candidates.definition: An Estate refers to all the possessions, property, and legal rights owned by an individual, including land, tenements, and personal assets. It also encompasses the total assets and liabilities left by a person at death, which are subject to distribution according to the terms of a will or the laws of intestacy.

candidates.label: Property Rights
candidates.definition: Property rights refer to the legal entitlements associated with the ownership, use, and disposition of property, including land, buildings, and other immovable assets. These rights generally include the ability to possess, control, lease, sell, and bequeath the property, subject to certain limitations imposed by law

candidates.label: Employment Transactions Practice
candidates.definition: Employment Transactions Practice refers to the area of law that involves the structuring, negotiation, and documentation of agreements related to employment relationships. This includes contracts, compensation plans, and other legal frameworks governing the employee-employer dynamic.

candidates.label: Clearing Title
candidates.definition: Clearing title refers to the process of resolving legal issues, claims, or encumbrances that could cloud the ownership status or legal rights associated with a particular property or piece of real estate. This process typically involves investigating and addressing any outstanding liens, disputes, or defects in the property's title to establish a clean and unambiguous ownership status. Clearing title is often necessary when buying or selling real estate to ensure that the buyer receives clear and marketable title to the property.

candidates.label: Tenant
candidates.definition: A person or entity who leases, rents, or occupies property (from a landlord).

candidates.label: Landlord
candidates.definition: One who lets land to another: the owner or holder of land or houses which he leases or rents to another. Seattle Hous. Auth. v. City of Seattle, 416 P.3d 1280 (Wash. App. 2018)

candidates.label: Landlord Tenant Law
candidates.definition: Law that details the rights and duties of landlords and tenants
synonyms: Direito do locador e do locatário

candidates.label: Landlord-Tenant Claims
candidates.definition: Landlord-Tenant Claims refer to legal disputes between a landlord and a tenant, including issues such as failure to return a tenant's security deposit, unlawful eviction, retaliation, and failure to repair or remedy.

candidates.label: Tenant
candidates.definition: A person or entity who leases, rents, or occupies property (from a landlord).

candidates.label: Landlord
candidates.definition: One who lets land to another: the owner or holder of land or houses which he leases or rents to another. Seattle Hous. Auth. v. City of Seattle, 416 P.3d 1280 (Wash. App. 2018)

candidates.label: Landlord Tenant Law
candidates.definition: Law that details the rights and duties of landlords and tenants
synonyms: Direito do locador e do locatário

candidates.label: Lease
candidates.definition: A Lease Agreement is a binding contract between a lessor (the owner) and a lessee (the renter) that stipulates the terms and conditions for renting out an asset, such as real estate property or an automobile. The agreement outlines details like the rental amount, lease duration, responsibilities of each party, and other specific provisions related to the rented asset.

candidates.label: Lease Agreements
candidates.definition: A Lease Related Agreement refers to any supplementary contract or document associated with a primary lease agreement, addressing specific terms, conditions, or scenarios that might arise during the lease period. Examples include lease renewals, extensions, terminations, subleases, assignments, and guaranties, among others. These agreements provide clarity and legal framework for particular aspects or contingencies of the lease relationship.

candidates.label: Agreements
candidates.definition: Agreements are written documents that establish a legally binding understanding between two or more parties, outlining the terms and conditions of a transaction or relationship.

candidates.label: Landlord
candidates.definition: One who lets land to another: the owner or holder of land or houses which he leases or rents to another. Seattle Hous. Auth. v. City of Seattle, 416 P.3d 1280 (Wash. App. 2018)

candidates.label: Landlord-Tenant Claims
candidates.definition: Landlord-Tenant Claims refer to legal disputes between a landlord and a tenant, including issues such as failure to return a tenant's security deposit, unlawful eviction, retaliation, and failure to repair or remedy.

candidates.label: Land Use and Zoning Law
candidates.definition: Law regulating the subdivision, use, and restriction of land.
synonyms: Derecho de uso del suelo y zonificación
Droit de l'utilisation des terres et de la zonification
Lei de Uso do Solo e Zoneamento

candidates.label: Construction and Development Law
candidates.definition: Law dealing with matters relating to building construction, engineering, and related fields.
synonyms: Derecho de la Construcción y Desarrollo
Droit de la construction et de l'aménagement

candidates.label: Violation
candidates.definition: A violation is an act or instance of breaking or failing to comply with a rule, regulation, contract, or law, which can lead to legal consequences.

candidates.label: Construction Defect Claims
candidates.definition: A construction defect claim is a legal action brought against a party for damages resulting from faulty construction work.
synonyms: 建築物の欠陥に関するクレーム

candidates.label: Foreclosure
candidates.definition: Foreclosure is the legal process by which a lender takes possession of a property, generally after the borrower fails to meet the financial obligations outlined in the mortgage agreement. The process usually culminates in the sale of the property, with the proceeds used to pay off the outstanding debt and any associated costs.

candidates.label: Lien
candidates.definition: A lien is a legal claim or right that a creditor has over a debtor's property as security for a debt or obligation, which can be enforced through court action or other legal means.

candidates.label: Loan
candidates.definition: A transaction whereby property is lent or given to another on condition of return or, where the loan is of money, repayment. In a loan of money, the money lent becomes the property of the borrower during the period of the loan against an undertaking to return a sum of an equivalent amount either on demand or on a specified date or in accordance with an agreed schedule of repayments.

candidates.label: Mortgage Foreclosure
candidates.definition: The concept of Mortgage Foreclosure refers to the legal process by which a lender takes possession of a property when the borrower fails to make mortgage payments, and sells it to recover the outstanding debt.
synonyms: 抵当フォアクロージャー

candidates.label: Foreclosure
candidates.definition: Foreclosure is the legal process by which a lender takes possession of a property, generally after the borrower fails to meet the financial obligations outlined in the mortgage agreement. The process usually culminates in the sale of the property, with the proceeds used to pay off the outstanding debt and any associated costs.

candidates.label: Reverse Mortgage Foreclosure
candidates.definition: Reverse Mortgage Foreclosure is the legal process in which a lender takes possession of a property after the borrower, typically an older homeowner, fails to meet the conditions of the reverse mortgage agreement. This can occur due to reasons like the death of the borrower, failure to pay property taxes or insurance, or permanently moving out of the home. The property is then sold to repay the loan balance and any associated costs

candidates.label: Eminent Domain Law
candidates.definition: Law regulating the taking of private land for public use.

candidates.label: Eminent Domain
candidates.definition: The concept of Eminent Domain (Taking) refers to the power of the government to take private property for public use, with just compensation to the owner, as authorized by the Constitution.

candidates.label: Reverse Mortgage Foreclosure
candidates.definition: Reverse Mortgage Foreclosure is the legal process in which a lender takes possession of a property after the borrower, typically an older homeowner, fails to meet the conditions of the reverse mortgage agreement. This can occur due to reasons like the death of the borrower, failure to pay property taxes or insurance, or permanently moving out of the home. The property is then sold to repay the loan balance and any associated costs

candidates.label: Mortgage Foreclosure
candidates.definition: The concept of Mortgage Foreclosure refers to the legal process by which a lender takes possession of a property when the borrower fails to make mortgage payments, and sells it to recover the outstanding debt.

The process usually culminates in the sale of the property, with the proceeds used to pay off the outstanding debt and any associated costs.
